chunk_summary: This section discusses lifestyle interventions and antihypertensive medication dosing for pediatric hypertension in metabolic risk clinics, emphasizing structured timelines, diagnostic evaluations, and comorbidity-driven medication choices.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of lifestyle interventions in managing pediatric hypertension.
Learn diagnostic timelines for elevated BP and stage 1 and 2 hypertension in children.
Identify first-line antihypertensive medications and their dosing for obesity-associated hypertension.
Recognize comorbidity-driven medication choices, such as ACE inhibitors for albuminuria or diabetes.
key_facts: Lifestyle therapy is first-line for elevated BP and uncomplicated stage 1 hypertension, with rechecks at 6 and 12 months.
Stage 2 hypertension requires repeat evaluation or referral within 1 week; symptomatic cases or pressures >30 mmHg above the 95th percentile need urgent care.
First-line oral antihypertensives include ACE inhibitors, ARBs, calcium channel blockers, and thiazide diuretics, with dosing tailored to age and comorbidities.
Enalapril dosing: start at 0.08 mg/kg/day (max 5 mg/day) and titrate up to 0.6 mg/kg/day (max 40 mg/day).
Lisinopril dosing: start at 0.07 mg/kg/day (max 5 mg/day) and titrate up to 0.6 mg/kg/day (max 40 mg/day).
Losartan dosing: start at 0.7 mg/kg (max 50 mg) and titrate up to 1.4 mg/kg (max 100 mg) daily.
reference_quote: Start medication when hypertension persists despite lifestyle, when hypertension is symptomatic, when stage 2 is present without a clearly modifiable factor, or when any stage of hypertension is present with diabetes or CKD. (American Academy of Pediatrics, 2017)
document_key_topics: Diagnostic timelines for elevated BP and hypertension stages
